aiMessages.content:
Build a Telegram AI agent called “FitBuddy X – Smart Fitness Coach”.

Goal:
Create an interactive fitness assistant that tracks user progress and motivates them daily.

Core Features:
1. Smart onboarding:
   - Ask name, age, goal (lose weight, gain muscle, stay fit)
   - Ask workout level (beginner, intermediate, advanced)
   - Ask preferred workout time

2. Add a level system:
   - Rookie
   - Challenger
   - Warrior
   - Beast Mode
Users level up after completing workouts.

3. 7-Day Fitness Challenge:
   - Provide daily workout plan
   - Provide nutrition tip
   - Track streak

4. Weekly AI summary report:
   - Show total workouts
   - Show streak
   - Give motivational analysis

5. Inline buttons:
   💪 Start Workout
   🔥 Daily Challenge
   🥗 Nutrition Tips
   📊 My Progress
   🏆 My Level

6. Personality:
Energetic, supportive, slightly humorous.
Act like a real personal trainer that adapts tone depending on user consistency.

Keep responses structured and motivating.

Created a fresh Telegram (grammY) project for FitBuddy X with MongoDB persistence, onboarding flow, home menu inline buttons, challenge start/complete flow, streak and level logic, and a periodic in-process scheduler loop. AI calls go through CookMyBots /chat with a runtime Bot Profile injected as the first system message, and logs were added for boot/env sanity, DB connect, scheduler cycles, and AI calls. Added /reset with confirmation and generated DOCS.md plus an updated .env.sample.

Files touched (23): .env.sample, DOCS.md, README.md, package.json, project.json, src/bot.js…
Validation: ✅ project passes syntax/basic checks after this edit.

planMarkdown:
Bot: FitBuddy X – Smart Fitness Coach (Telegram AI Agent)

Summary:
FitBuddy X is a production-ready Telegram bot that behaves like an energetic personal trainer: it onboards users, delivers a 7-day challenge with daily workouts and nutrition tips, tracks workouts and streaks, levels users up, and produces a weekly summary report with motivational analysis.

The bot runs as a single Node.js (ESM) service using grammY. MongoDB is used for persistence (profiles, challenge state, workouts, streaks, level progress, and scheduled report bookkeeping). The design emphasizes reliable state handling, idempotent daily/weekly deliveries, clear user interaction via inline buttons, and safe AI usage with consistent structured outputs and guardrails.

Product goals:
FitBuddy X should help users build consistency through short daily actions, visible progress, and supportive accountability. The product goal is not to be a full exercise library, but to make it easy to start, complete, and track workouts in a lightweight Telegram experience.

Primary outcomes:
1) Increase adherence: daily challenge prompts, streak tracking, and simple completion flows.
2) Increase motivation: leveling system, humor, and adaptive tone that responds to consistency.
3) Increase clarity: structured workout steps, time estimates, rest guidance, and simple nutrition tips.

User promises:
1) A fast onboarding that leads to a personalized plan immediately.
2) One tap to start a workout, one tap to complete it.
3) Progress always available and easy to understand (streak, totals, level, badges).
4) Weekly report that feels personal and constructive, not generic.

Scope boundaries (for production readiness):
1) Provide safe, general fitness guidance with disclaimers; avoid medical advice.
2) Use bodyweight or minimal-equipment routines by default; optionally ask equipment later as an enhancement.
3) Do not require external integrations for wearables or calendars.

High-level architecture:
Runtime and modules:
1) Single Node.js (ESM) process using grammY.
2) Update handling via webhook or long polling depending on deployment; both supported by the same service.
3) MongoDB for durable state; minimal in-memory caching for hot reads only.

Bot interaction layers:
1) Router layer: grammY middleware routes updates into command handlers, callback query handlers (inline buttons), and conversational onboarding.
2) Conversation/state layer: use a conversation approach for onboarding and multi-step flows, backed by MongoDB so it survives restarts.
3) Domain services layer: focused services for user profiles, challenge plan generation, workout logging, streak computation, leveling, and report generation.
4) AI layer (optional but implied by “AI agent”): a constrained generation service that produces workout text, nutrition tips, and weekly report narrative in a strict structured format. The AI layer should be wrapped with:
   1) deterministic prompts that enforce concise sections
   2) validation of outputs (length limits, required fields)
   3) fallbacks to template-based content if generation fails

Scheduling within a single process:
1) A lightweight internal scheduler loop runs periodic jobs (every minute) to:
   1) deliver daily challenge messages at a user’s preferred time
   2) generate and send weekly reports
2) The scheduler is driven by MongoDB job records to ensure idempotency and recovery after restarts.

Idempotency and correctness:
1) Every “send daily challenge” and “send weekly report” action is recorded with a unique key (userId + date or week). If already sent, skip.
2) Workout completion events are stored as immutable logs; totals and streaks are computed from logs to avoid corruption.
3) Callback queries always verify current user state and ignore stale buttons.

Personality and tone engine:
1) Tone is derived from recent behavior signals (streak, last workout date, missed days).
2) The bot adapts messaging intensity:
   1) Consistent users: more playful, push for progression
   2) Inconsistent users: more supportive, shorter workouts, emphasize restart without guilt
3) Personality is implemented as templates plus AI “style” constraints, not freeform improvisation.

Safety:
1) Include a short disclaimer during onboarding and before first workout: general fitness guidance, consult a professional if needed.
2) Avoid suggesting unsafe exercises for minors or users who indicate injuries; optionally add an “injuries/limitations” question in onboarding v1.1.
3) Ensure the AI never claims to diagnose or treat health conditions.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (suggested). All documents include createdAt/updatedAt timestamps.

1) users
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) name
3) age
4) goal (loseweight | gainmuscle | stayfit)
5) level (beginner | intermediate | advanced)
6) preferredWorkoutTime (HH:mm, user-local)
7) timezoneOffsetMinutes (captured from user input flow; Telegram does not reliably provide timezone)
8) personalityPrefs (optional: humor level, message length)
9) safetyFlags (optional: minor, injuryNotes, contraindications)
10) currentRank (Rookie | Challenger | Warrior | Beast Mode)
11) xp (numeric)
12) onboardingStatus (notstarted | inprogress | completed)

2) workouts
Immutable workout completion log.
1) telegramUserId
2) dateKey (YYYY-MM-DD in user-local time)
3) challengeDay (1..7 or null)
4) workoutType (strength | cardio | mobility | mixed)
5) durationMinutes (estimated or user-confirmed)
6) intensity (easy | moderate | hard)
7) completed (boolean)
8) notes (optional user feedback)

3) challengestate
Tracks the 7-day challenge lifecycle.
1) telegramUserId
2) active (boolean)
3) startDateKey
4) currentDay (1..7)
5) lastDeliveredDateKey
6) lastCompletedDateKey
7) streakCount
8) longestStreak
9) planVersion (to support updates)
10) dayPlans (optional cached content: workout + nutrition tip per day)

4) deliveries
Idempotent record of outbound content.
1) telegramUserId
2) type (dailychallenge | nutritiontip | weeklyreport | nudge)
3) key (for example: dailychallenge:YYYY-MM-DD or weeklyreport:YYYY-WW)
4) status (sent | failed)
5) messageId (Telegram message id if available)
6) error (if failed)

5) jobs
Scheduler-driven jobs.
1) jobType (senddaily | sendweekly)
2) telegramUserId
3) runAt (timestamp)
4) key (same idempotency key)
5) status (pending | running | done | failed)
6) attempts
7) lastError

6) aiartifacts (optional)
Stores prompts and outputs for audits and debugging.
1) telegramUserId
2) artifactType (dayplan | weeklyreport)
3) key (date/week)
4) inputSummary (sanitized)
5) output (structured text)
6) modelMetadata (name, latency)

Derived metrics (computed, not stored unless needed):
1) Total workouts in last 7 days, last 30 days
2) Current streak and longest streak
3) XP progress to next rank

Level system design:
1) XP per completed workout: base value (for example 10) plus modifiers for streak milestone or intensity.
2) Rank thresholds:
   1) Rookie: 0+
   2) Challenger: threshold 1
   3) Warrior: threshold 2
   4) Beast Mode: threshold 3
3) Thresholds should be configurable in environment or a config collection to tune without code changes.

Key flows:
1) Start and onboarding (/start)
1) User taps /start.
2) Bot checks onboardingStatus.
3) If not completed, begins a guided multi-step conversation:
   1) Ask name
   2) Ask age (validate numeric range; if under a minimum age, restrict intensity and add extra safety messaging)
   3) Ask goal (inline buttons)
   4) Ask workout level (inline buttons)
   5) Ask preferred workout time (ask for HH:mm and clarify it is local time)
   6) Ask timezone offset (simple selection: “I’m in UTC+X”; keep minimal to reduce friction)
4) Bot confirms profile summary and offers primary buttons.
5) Bot auto-enrolls user into the 7-day challenge (or asks to start now).

2) Home menu (inline buttons)
Persistent keyboard or inline menu message with:
1) Start Workout
2) Daily Challenge
3) Nutrition Tips
4) My Progress
5) My Level
Each button triggers a callback query that routes to the relevant handler. The handler edits the menu message or sends a new structured message to reduce clutter.

3) Daily challenge delivery
1) Scheduler evaluates which users are due at current minute based on preferredWorkoutTime and timezone.
2) For each due user, it checks deliveries for the dateKey to prevent duplicates.
3) It sends:
   1) Day number (1..7)
   2) Workout plan (warm-up, main sets, cooldown, estimated duration)
   3) Nutrition tip (simple, safe, actionable)
   4) Buttons: Start Workout, Mark as Done, Swap/Modify (optional), Remind me later (optional)
4) It records a deliveries entry with messageId.

4) Start workout (button: Start Workout)
1) Bot posts the workout steps in a compact checklist style.
2) Bot provides pacing guidance (rest times, form reminders) and a single “Complete Workout” button.
3) Optionally ask for perceived effort (easy/moderate/hard) after completion to personalize future difficulty.
4) On completion:
   1) Insert workouts log entry (immutable)
   2) Update challengestate streak fields
   3) Add XP and check for rank-up
   4) Send a short celebratory message with tone based on consistency

5) Mark as done (button: Mark as Done)
A fast path for users who did the workout but don’t want the full walkthrough.
1) Create workout completion log with minimal fields.
2) Apply streak and XP updates.
3) Respond with a concise congratulatory message.

6) Nutrition tips (button: Nutrition Tips)
1) Provide a single tip per day, aligned to user goal.
2) Keep tips non-medical and practical.
3) Offer: “Give me another tip” with rate limits, and “Save this tip” (optional, stored in user profile).

7) My progress (button: My Progress)
Show structured snapshot:
1) Current streak and longest streak
2) Workouts completed this week
3) Challenge day status (completed/pending)
4) XP and rank progress
5) Last workout date
Offer buttons: View weekly report (if available), Start workout.

8) My level (button: My Level)
1) Show rank name and what it means.
2) Show XP and next threshold.
3) Show recent achievements (streak milestones, weekly completion badges).

9) Weekly AI summary report
1) Once per week at a configured time (user-local), scheduler creates a sendweekly job.
2) Build a weekly summary input from workouts logs:
   1) total workouts
   2) streak metrics
   3) missed days count
   4) any user feedback on intensity
3) Generate report narrative using AI under strict structure:
   1) Highlights
   2) Consistency score
   3) What to improve next week
   4) Motivation line in FitBuddy X voice
4) Send report and record deliveries.

10) Tone adaptation rules
1) If streak increased or user completed 2+ workouts in last 3 days: upbeat, playful challenge.
2) If no workouts in 3+ days: supportive re-entry, offer a shorter “comeback workout”.
3) If user repeatedly misses preferred time: suggest adjusting preferred time.

11) Failure and fallback behaviors
1) If AI generation fails: use prewritten templates by goal and level.
2) If MongoDB temporarily unavailable: bot replies with a short “try again” message and does not lose user data (ensure writes are retried by request-level logic).
3) If Telegram send fails: mark delivery failed and retry via jobs with backoff and max attempts.

Tech stack:
Core runtime:
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot framework.
3) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN read from environment variables.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB for all durable state (users, workouts, challenge state, deliveries, jobs, AI artifacts).
2) In-memory maps allowed only for ephemeral caches (for example, per-process rate limiting counters), not for core state.

AI integration:
1) An external AI text generation API can be used to produce structured plans and weekly reports.
2) The AI layer must be optional behind a feature flag; templates should cover the full functionality if AI is unavailable.
3) Store minimal AI artifacts for debugging while avoiding sensitive data.

Storage:
1) If later adding progress images, meal photos, or PDFs, store in object storage or simple filesystem storage, with MongoDB storing metadata and references.

Deployment and operations:
1) Run as a Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Configure environment variables for token, MongoDB URI, feature flags, and schedule parameters.
3) Use webhook mode when a public HTTPS endpoint is available; otherwise long polling.

Observability:
1) Structured logging (request id, telegramUserId, handler name, latency).
2) Basic metrics counters (messages processed, sends failed, job retries) logged or stored in MongoDB for simple dashboards.

Security:
1) Keep secrets only in environment variables.
2) Validate and sanitize all user inputs.
3) Implement per-user rate limits for AI-heavy endpoints and “another tip” loops.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and idempotency:
1) Daily/weekly messages must never double-send; deliveries keys enforce this.
2) Scheduler jobs must be restart-safe; jobs collection holds pending work and retry state.
3) All callback actions should be idempotent where possible (for example, completing the same workout twice should be detected by dateKey and challengeDay).

Performance:
1) Keep messages concise to reduce Telegram API overhead.
2) Use indexed queries in MongoDB for telegramUserId, dateKey, deliveries key, jobs runAt.
3) Batch scheduler lookups: query jobs due in the next minute rather than scanning all users.

Privacy:
1) Store only what is needed: basic profile fields and workout logs.
2) Provide a /deletemydata command that purges user data.
3) Avoid storing raw chat history; store only structured summaries when needed.

Safety and compliance posture:
1) Clear disclaimers: general fitness guidance, not medical advice.
2) Conservative defaults: beginner-friendly routines unless user chooses higher intensity.
3) If user indicates injury or pain, recommend stopping and seeking professional advice; provide low-impact alternatives.

Maintainability:
1) Version plan templates (planVersion) to allow updating routines without breaking existing users.
2) Keep domain logic testable (streak calculation, XP thresholds, scheduling key generation).
3) Use feature flags for AI generation, reminders, and extended onboarding.

User experience quality:
1) Structured responses with consistent sections: Today’s workout, Nutrition tip, Quick win, Buttons.
2) Use humor lightly and avoid shaming language.
3) Offer “Remind me later” and “Adjust my time” to reduce churn.

Abuse prevention:
1) Rate limit repeated button taps.
2) Detect spammy usage patterns and degrade gracefully (short replies, block AI calls).
3) Handle group chats by defaulting to private chat onboarding (ask user to DM the bot).

Bot commands:
• /start — Start FitBuddy X, run onboarding, and show the main menu. (usage: /start) [Getting started]
• /menu — Show the main action buttons (workout, challenge, nutrition, progress, level). (usage: /menu) [Navigation]
• /challenge — View or start the 7-day fitness challenge and see today’s plan. (usage: /challenge) [Challenge]
• /workout — Start today’s workout (or a quick fallback workout if none scheduled). (usage: /workout) [Workouts]
• /done — Mark today’s workout as completed (fast path). (usage: /done) [Workouts]
• /nutrition — Get today’s nutrition tip tailored to your goal. (usage: /nutrition) [Nutrition]
• /progress — Show streak, totals, and recent activity. (usage: /progress) [Progress]
• /level — Show your rank, XP, and what’s needed to level up. (usage: /level) [Progress]
• /settime — Update your preferred workout time and timezone offset for reminders. (usage: /settime) [Settings]
• /profile — View and edit your profile (goal, level, age). (usage: /profile) [Settings]
• /weekly — View your latest weekly summary report (if available). (usage: /weekly) [Reports]
• /help — Explain how to use FitBuddy X and what commands/buttons do. (usage: /help) [Support]
• /deletemydata — Permanently delete your FitBuddy X data (profile and logs). (usage: /deletemydata) [Privacy]

DOCS.md.content:
FitBuddy X – Smart Fitness Coach

What it does

FitBuddy X is an interactive Telegram fitness assistant. It onboards you, runs a 7-day challenge, tracks workouts and streaks, calculates your level, and sends a weekly AI summary report.

Public commands

1) /start
Starts the bot. If you have not completed onboarding, it will ask a few questions (name, age, goal, workout level, preferred workout time). If onboarding is complete, it shows the home menu.

2) /help
Shows how to use the bot and what each menu button does.

3) /reset
Resets your FitBuddy X data (profile, progress, challenge state, workout logs). The bot will ask for confirmation.

Home menu buttons

1) 💪 Start Workout
Gives you a quick workout (even if you are not in the challenge). You can mark it completed and optionally add a note.

2) 🔥 Daily Challenge
Starts or continues a 7-day challenge. If active, it shows today’s plan and provides buttons to complete, swap, skip, or end.

3) 🥗 Nutrition Tips
Shows a simple nutrition tip tailored to your goal and level.

4) 📊 My Progress
Shows total workouts, current streak, best streak, challenge status, and this week’s count. Includes a button to generate the weekly summary on demand.

5) 🏆 My Level
Shows your current level and how many workouts remain until the next level.

Weekly scheduling

The bot runs an in-process scheduler loop. Every cycle it checks which users are due for a weekly AI summary report and sends it at most once per week per user. The bot tracks lastWeeklyReportAt in the progress collection.

Environment variables

Required

1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
Telegram bot token.

2) MONGODB_URI
MongoDB connection string used to store user profiles, progress, challenge state, and workout logs.

3) COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT
CookMyBots AI Gateway base URL. Example: https://api.cookmybots.com/api/ai

4) COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY
CookMyBots AI Gateway key.

Optional

1) TZ
Default timezone used when a user has not configured a timezone. Default is UTC.

2) PORT
Reserved for hosting environments. Default is 3000. The bot does not require HTTP.

3) BASE_URL
Optional display/logging value.

How to test quickly

1) /start
Complete onboarding.

2) Tap 🔥 Daily Challenge
Start the challenge, then tap ✅ Mark Completed.

3) Tap 📊 My Progress
Verify totals and streak.

4) Tap 📩 Get Weekly Summary Now
Verify the AI report is generated.

README.md.content:
FitBuddy X – Smart Fitness Coach

This is a Telegram bot (grammY) that onboards users, runs a 7-day fitness challenge, tracks workouts and streaks, awards levels, and sends weekly AI summary reports.

Setup

1) Install
npm install

2) Configure env
Copy .env.sample to .env and fill in the values.

3) Run locally
npm run dev

4) Run production
npm start

Notes

1) The bot uses long polling by default.
2) MongoDB is required for persistence (profiles, progress, challenge state, workout logs, weekly reports).
3) AI calls go through the CookMyBots AI Gateway (COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT + COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY).

Commands

1) /start
2) /help
3) /reset

Troubleshooting

1) If you see TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is required, set it in your environment.
2) If MongoDB is not configured, the bot will warn and fall back to limited in-memory behavior.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: FitBuddy X – Smart Fitness Coach (Telegram AI Agent)

Summary:
FitBuddy X is a production-ready Telegram bot that behaves like an energetic personal trainer: it onboards users, delivers a 7-day challenge with daily workouts and nutrition tips, tracks workouts and streaks, levels users up, and produces a weekly summary report with motivational analysis.

The bot runs as a single Node.js (ESM) service using grammY. MongoDB is used for persistence (profiles, challenge state, workouts, streaks, level progress, and scheduled report bookkeeping). The design emphasizes reliable state handling, idempotent daily/weekly deliveries, clear user interaction via inline buttons, and safe AI usage with consistent structured outputs and guardrails.

Product goals:
FitBuddy X should help users build consistency through short daily actions, visible progress, and supportive accountability. The product goal is not to be a full exercise library, but to make it easy to start, complete, and track workouts in a lightweight Telegram experience.

Primary outcomes:
1) Increase adherence: daily challenge prompts, streak tracking, and simple completion flows.
2) Increase motivation: leveling system, humor, and adaptive tone that responds to consistency.
3) Increase clarity: structured workout steps, time estimates, rest guidance, and simple nutrition tips.

User promises:
1) A fast onboarding that leads to a personalized plan immediately.
2) One tap to start a workout, one tap to complete it.
3) Progress always available and easy to understand (streak, totals, level, badges).
4) Weekly report that feels personal and constructive, not generic.

Scope boundaries (for production readiness):
1) Provide safe, general fitness guidance with disclaimers; avoid medical advice.
2) Use bodyweight or minimal-equipment routines by default; optionally ask equipment later as an enhancement.
3) Do not require external integrations for wearables or calendars.

High-level architecture:
Runtime and modules:
1) Single Node.js (ESM) process using grammY.
2) Update handling via webhook or long polling depending on deployment; both supported by the same service.
3) MongoDB for durable state; minimal in-memory caching for hot reads only.

Bot interaction layers:
1) Router layer: grammY middleware routes updates into command handlers, callback query handlers (inline buttons), and conversational onboarding.
2) Conversation/state layer: use a conversation approach for onboarding and multi-step flows, backed by MongoDB so it survives restarts.
3) Domain services layer: focused services for user profiles, challenge plan generation, workout logging, streak computation, leveling, and report generation.
4) AI layer (optional but implied by “AI agent”): a constrained generation service that produces workout text, nutrition tips, and weekly report narrative in a strict structured format. The AI layer should be wrapped with:
   1) deterministic prompts that enforce concise sections
   2) validation of outputs (length limits, required fields)
   3) fallbacks to template-based content if generation fails

Scheduling within a single process:
1) A lightweight internal scheduler loop runs periodic jobs (every minute) to:
   1) deliver daily challenge messages at a user’s preferred time
   2) generate and send weekly reports
2) The scheduler is driven by MongoDB job records to ensure idempotency and recovery after restarts.

Idempotency and correctness:
1) Every “send daily challenge” and “send weekly report” action is recorded with a unique key (userId + date or week). If already sent, skip.
2) Workout completion events are stored as immutable logs; totals and streaks are computed from logs to avoid corruption.
3) Callback queries always verify current user state and ignore stale buttons.

Personality and tone engine:
1) Tone is derived from recent behavior signals (streak, last workout date, missed days).
2) The bot adapts messaging intensity:
   1) Consistent users: more playful, push for progression
   2) Inconsistent users: more supportive, shorter workouts, emphasize restart without guilt
3) Personality is implemented as templates plus AI “style” constraints, not freeform improvisation.

Safety:
1) Include a short disclaimer during onboarding and before first workout: general fitness guidance, consult a professional if needed.
2) Avoid suggesting unsafe exercises for minors or users who indicate injuries; optionally add an “injuries/limitations” question in onboarding v1.1.
3) Ensure the AI never claims to diagnose or treat health conditions.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (suggested). All documents include createdAt/updatedAt timestamps.

1) users
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) name
3) age
4) goal (loseweight | gainmuscle | stayfit)
5) level (beginner | intermediate | advanced)
6) preferredWorkoutTime (HH:mm, user-local)
7) timezoneOffsetMinutes (captured from user input flow; Telegram does not reliably provide timezone)
8) personalityPrefs (optional: humor level, message length)
9) safetyFlags (optional: minor, injuryNotes, contraindications)
10) currentRank (Rookie | Challenger | Warrior | Beast Mode)
11) xp (numeric)
12) onboardingStatus (notstarted | inprogress | completed)

2) workouts
Immutable workout completion log.
1) telegramUserId
2) dateKey (YYYY-MM-DD in user-local time)
3) challengeDay (1..7 or null)
4) workoutType (strength | cardio | mobility | mixed)
5) durationMinutes (estimated or user-confirmed)
6) intensity (easy | moderate | hard)
7) completed (boolean)
8) notes (optional user feedback)

3) challengestate
Tracks the 7-day challenge lifecycle.
1) telegramUserId
2) active (boolean)
3) startDateKey
4) currentDay (1..7)
5) lastDeliveredDateKey
6) lastCompletedDateKey
7) streakCount
8) longestStreak
9) planVersion (to support updates)
10) dayPlans (optional cached content: workout + nutrition tip per day)

4) deliveries
Idempotent record of outbound content.
1) telegramUserId
2) type (dailychallenge | nutritiontip | weeklyreport | nudge)
3) key (for example: dailychallenge:YYYY-MM-DD or weeklyreport:YYYY-WW)
4) status (sent | failed)
5) messageId (Telegram message id if available)
6) error (if failed)

5) jobs
Scheduler-driven jobs.
1) jobType (senddaily | sendweekly)
2) telegramUserId
3) runAt (timestamp)
4) key (same idempotency key)
5) status (pending | running | done | failed)
6) attempts
7) lastError

6) aiartifacts (optional)
Stores prompts and outputs for audits and debugging.
1) telegramUserId
2) artifactType (dayplan | weeklyreport)
3) key (date/week)
4) inputSummary (sanitized)
5) output (structured text)
6) modelMetadata (name, latency)

Derived metrics (computed, not stored unless needed):
1) Total workouts in last 7 days, last 30 days
2) Current streak and longest streak
3) XP progress to next rank

Level system design:
1) XP per completed workout: base value (for example 10) plus modifiers for streak milestone or intensity.
2) Rank thresholds:
   1) Rookie: 0+
   2) Challenger: threshold 1
   3) Warrior: threshold 2
   4) Beast Mode: threshold 3
3) Thresholds should be configurable in environment or a config collection to tune without code changes.

Key flows:
1) Start and onboarding (/start)
1) User taps /start.
2) Bot checks onboardingStatus.
3) If not completed, begins a guided multi-step conversation:
   1) Ask name
   2) Ask age (validate numeric range; if under a minimum age, restrict intensity and add extra safety messaging)
   3) Ask goal (inline buttons)
   4) Ask workout level (inline buttons)
   5) Ask preferred workout time (ask for HH:mm and clarify it is local time)
   6) Ask timezone offset (simple selection: “I’m in UTC+X”; keep minimal to reduce friction)
4) Bot confirms profile summary and offers primary buttons.
5) Bot auto-enrolls user into the 7-day challenge (or asks to start now).

2) Home menu (inline buttons)
Persistent keyboard or inline menu message with:
1) Start Workout
2) Daily Challenge
3) Nutrition Tips
4) My Progress
5) My Level
Each button triggers a callback query that routes to the relevant handler. The handler edits the menu message or sends a new structured message to reduce clutter.

3) Daily challenge delivery
1) Scheduler evaluates which users are due at current minute based on preferredWorkoutTime and timezone.
2) For each due user, it checks deliveries for the dateKey to prevent duplicates.
3) It sends:
   1) Day number (1..7)
   2) Workout plan (warm-up, main sets, cooldown, estimated duration)
   3) Nutrition tip (simple, safe, actionable)
   4) Buttons: Start Workout, Mark as Done, Swap/Modify (optional), Remind me later (optional)
4) It records a deliveries entry with messageId.

4) Start workout (button: Start Workout)
1) Bot posts the workout steps in a compact checklist style.
2) Bot provides pacing guidance (rest times, form reminders) and a single “Complete Workout” button.
3) Optionally ask for perceived effort (easy/moderate/hard) after completion to personalize future difficulty.
4) On completion:
   1) Insert workouts log entry (immutable)
   2) Update challengestate streak fields
   3) Add XP and check for rank-up
   4) Send a short celebratory message with tone based on consistency

5) Mark as done (button: Mark as Done)
A fast path for users who did the workout but don’t want the full walkthrough.
1) Create workout completion log with minimal fields.
2) Apply streak and XP updates.
3) Respond with a concise congratulatory message.

6) Nutrition tips (button: Nutrition Tips)
1) Provide a single tip per day, aligned to user goal.
2) Keep tips non-medical and practical.
3) Offer: “Give me another tip” with rate limits, and “Save this tip” (optional, stored in user profile).

7) My progress (button: My Progress)
Show structured snapshot:
1) Current streak and longest streak
2) Workouts completed this week
3) Challenge day status (completed/pending)
4) XP and rank progress
5) Last workout date
Offer buttons: View weekly report (if available), Start workout.

8) My level (button: My Level)
1) Show rank name and what it means.
2) Show XP and next threshold.
3) Show recent achievements (streak milestones, weekly completion badges).

9) Weekly AI summary report
1) Once per week at a configured time (user-local), scheduler creates a sendweekly job.
2) Build a weekly summary input from workouts logs:
   1) total workouts
   2) streak metrics
   3) missed days count
   4) any user feedback on intensity
3) Generate report narrative using AI under strict structure:
   1) Highlights
   2) Consistency score
   3) What to improve next week
   4) Motivation line in FitBuddy X voice
4) Send report and record deliveries.

10) Tone adaptation rules
1) If streak increased or user completed 2+ workouts in last 3 days: upbeat, playful challenge.
2) If no workouts in 3+ days: supportive re-entry, offer a shorter “comeback workout”.
3) If user repeatedly misses preferred time: suggest adjusting preferred time.

11) Failure and fallback behaviors
1) If AI generation fails: use prewritten templates by goal and level.
2) If MongoDB temporarily unavailable: bot replies with a short “try again” message and does not lose user data (ensure writes are retried by request-level logic).
3) If Telegram send fails: mark delivery failed and retry via jobs with backoff and max attempts.

Tech stack:
Core runtime:
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot framework.
3) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN read from environment variables.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB for all durable state (users, workouts, challenge state, deliveries, jobs, AI artifacts).
2) In-memory maps allowed only for ephemeral caches (for example, per-process rate limiting counters), not for core state.

AI integration:
1) An external AI text generation API can be used to produce structured plans and weekly reports.
2) The AI layer must be optional behind a feature flag; templates should cover the full functionality if AI is unavailable.
3) Store minimal AI artifacts for debugging while avoiding sensitive data.

Storage:
1) If later adding progress images, meal photos, or PDFs, store in object storage or simple filesystem storage, with MongoDB storing metadata and references.

Deployment and operations:
1) Run as a Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Configure environment variables for token, MongoDB URI, feature flags, and schedule parameters.
3) Use webhook mode when a public HTTPS endpoint is available; otherwise long polling.

Observability:
1) Structured logging (request id, telegramUserId, handler name, latency).
2) Basic metrics counters (messages processed, sends failed, job retries) logged or stored in MongoDB for simple dashboards.

Security:
1) Keep secrets only in environment variables.
2) Validate and sanitize all user inputs.
3) Implement per-user rate limits for AI-heavy endpoints and “another tip” loops.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and idempotency:
1) Daily/weekly messages must never double-send; deliveries keys enforce this.
2) Scheduler jobs must be restart-safe; jobs collection holds pending work and retry state.
3) All callback actions should be idempotent where possible (for example, completing the same workout twice should be detected by dateKey and challengeDay).

1) Keep messages concise to reduce Telegram API overhead.

3) Batch scheduler lookups: query jobs due in the next minute rather than scanning all users.

3) Avoid storing raw chat history; store only structured summaries when needed.

2) Conservative defaults: beginner-friendly routines unless user chooses higher intensity.
3) If user indicates injury or pain, recommend stopping and seeking professional advice; provide low-impact alternatives.

1) Version plan templates (planVersion) to allow updating routines without breaking existing users.
2) Keep domain logic testable (streak calculation, XP thresholds, scheduling key generation).
3) Use feature flags for AI generation, reminders, and extended onboarding.

User experience quality:
1) Structured responses with consistent sections: Today’s workout, Nutrition tip, Quick win, Buttons.
2) Use humor lightly and avoid shaming language.
3) Offer “Remind me later” and “Adjust my time” to reduce churn.

1) Rate limit repeated button taps.
2) Detect spammy usage patterns and degrade gracefully (short replies, block AI calls).
3) Handle group chats by defaulting to private chat onboarding (ask user to DM the bot).

Bot commands:
• /start — Start FitBuddy X, run onboarding, and show the main menu. (usage: /start) [Getting started]

• /progress — Show streak, totals, and recent activity. (usage: /progress) [Progress]
• /level — Show your rank, XP, and what’s needed to level up. (usage: /level) [Progress]
• /settime — Update your preferred workout time and timezone offset for reminders. (usage: /settime) [Settings]
• /profile — View and edit your profile (goal, level, age). (usage: /profile) [Settings]
• /weekly — View your latest weekly summary report (if available). (usage: /weekly) [Reports]
• /help — Explain how to use FitBuddy X and what commands/buttons do. (usage: /help) [Support]
• /deletemydata — Permanently delete your FitBuddy X data (profile and logs). (usage: /deletemydata) [Privacy]

planSections.productGoals:
FitBuddy X should help users build consistency through short daily actions, visible progress, and supportive accountability. The product goal is not to be a full exercise library, but to make it easy to start, complete, and track workouts in a lightweight Telegram experience.

Primary outcomes:
1) Increase adherence: daily challenge prompts, streak tracking, and simple completion flows.
2) Increase motivation: leveling system, humor, and adaptive tone that responds to consistency.
3) Increase clarity: structured workout steps, time estimates, rest guidance, and simple nutrition tips.

User promises:
1) A fast onboarding that leads to a personalized plan immediately.
2) One tap to start a workout, one tap to complete it.
3) Progress always available and easy to understand (streak, totals, level, badges).
4) Weekly report that feels personal and constructive, not generic.

Scope boundaries (for production readiness):
1) Provide safe, general fitness guidance with disclaimers; avoid medical advice.
2) Use bodyweight or minimal-equipment routines by default; optionally ask equipment later as an enhancement.
3) Do not require external integrations for wearables or calendars.
planSections.highLevelArchitecture:
Runtime and modules:
1) Single Node.js (ESM) process using grammY.
2) Update handling via webhook or long polling depending on deployment; both supported by the same service.
3) MongoDB for durable state; minimal in-memory caching for hot reads only.

Bot interaction layers:
1) Router layer: grammY middleware routes updates into command handlers, callback query handlers (inline buttons), and conversational onboarding.
2) Conversation/state layer: use a conversation approach for onboarding and multi-step flows, backed by MongoDB so it survives restarts.
3) Domain services layer: focused services for user profiles, challenge plan generation, workout logging, streak computation, leveling, and report generation.
4) AI layer (optional but implied by “AI agent”): a constrained generation service that produces workout text, nutrition tips, and weekly report narrative in a strict structured format. The AI layer should be wrapped with:
   1) deterministic prompts that enforce concise sections
   2) validation of outputs (length limits, required fields)
   3) fallbacks to template-based content if generation fails

Scheduling within a single process:
1) A lightweight internal scheduler loop runs periodic jobs (every minute) to:
   1) deliver daily challenge messages at a user’s preferred time
   2) generate and send weekly reports
2) The scheduler is driven by MongoDB job records to ensure idempotency and recovery after restarts.

Idempotency and correctness:
1) Every “send daily challenge” and “send weekly report” action is recorded with a unique key (userId + date or week). If already sent, skip.
2) Workout completion events are stored as immutable logs; totals and streaks are computed from logs to avoid corruption.
3) Callback queries always verify current user state and ignore stale buttons.

Personality and tone engine:
1) Tone is derived from recent behavior signals (streak, last workout date, missed days).
2) The bot adapts messaging intensity:
   1) Consistent users: more playful, push for progression
   2) Inconsistent users: more supportive, shorter workouts, emphasize restart without guilt
3) Personality is implemented as templates plus AI “style” constraints, not freeform improvisation.

Safety:
1) Include a short disclaimer during onboarding and before first workout: general fitness guidance, consult a professional if needed.
2) Avoid suggesting unsafe exercises for minors or users who indicate injuries; optionally add an “injuries/limitations” question in onboarding v1.1.
3) Ensure the AI never claims to diagnose or treat health conditions.
planSections.dataModel:
MongoDB collections (suggested). All documents include createdAt/updatedAt timestamps.

1) users
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) name
3) age
4) goal (loseweight | gainmuscle | stayfit)
5) level (beginner | intermediate | advanced)
6) preferredWorkoutTime (HH:mm, user-local)
7) timezoneOffsetMinutes (captured from user input flow; Telegram does not reliably provide timezone)
8) personalityPrefs (optional: humor level, message length)
9) safetyFlags (optional: minor, injuryNotes, contraindications)
10) currentRank (Rookie | Challenger | Warrior | Beast Mode)
11) xp (numeric)
12) onboardingStatus (notstarted | inprogress | completed)

2) workouts
Immutable workout completion log.
1) telegramUserId
2) dateKey (YYYY-MM-DD in user-local time)
3) challengeDay (1..7 or null)
4) workoutType (strength | cardio | mobility | mixed)
5) durationMinutes (estimated or user-confirmed)
6) intensity (easy | moderate | hard)
7) completed (boolean)
8) notes (optional user feedback)

3) challengestate
Tracks the 7-day challenge lifecycle.
1) telegramUserId
2) active (boolean)
3) startDateKey
4) currentDay (1..7)
5) lastDeliveredDateKey
6) lastCompletedDateKey
7) streakCount
8) longestStreak
9) planVersion (to support updates)
10) dayPlans (optional cached content: workout + nutrition tip per day)

4) deliveries
Idempotent record of outbound content.
1) telegramUserId
2) type (dailychallenge | nutritiontip | weeklyreport | nudge)
3) key (for example: dailychallenge:YYYY-MM-DD or weeklyreport:YYYY-WW)
4) status (sent | failed)
5) messageId (Telegram message id if available)
6) error (if failed)

5) jobs
Scheduler-driven jobs.
1) jobType (senddaily | sendweekly)
2) telegramUserId
3) runAt (timestamp)
4) key (same idempotency key)
5) status (pending | running | done | failed)
6) attempts
7) lastError

6) aiartifacts (optional)
Stores prompts and outputs for audits and debugging.
1) telegramUserId
2) artifactType (dayplan | weeklyreport)
3) key (date/week)
4) inputSummary (sanitized)
5) output (structured text)
6) modelMetadata (name, latency)

Derived metrics (computed, not stored unless needed):
1) Total workouts in last 7 days, last 30 days
2) Current streak and longest streak
3) XP progress to next rank

Level system design:
1) XP per completed workout: base value (for example 10) plus modifiers for streak milestone or intensity.
2) Rank thresholds:
   1) Rookie: 0+
   2) Challenger: threshold 1
   3) Warrior: threshold 2
   4) Beast Mode: threshold 3
3) Thresholds should be configurable in environment or a config collection to tune without code changes.
planSections.keyFlows:
1) Start and onboarding (/start)
1) User taps /start.
2) Bot checks onboardingStatus.
3) If not completed, begins a guided multi-step conversation:
   1) Ask name
   2) Ask age (validate numeric range; if under a minimum age, restrict intensity and add extra safety messaging)
   3) Ask goal (inline buttons)
   4) Ask workout level (inline buttons)
   5) Ask preferred workout time (ask for HH:mm and clarify it is local time)
   6) Ask timezone offset (simple selection: “I’m in UTC+X”; keep minimal to reduce friction)
4) Bot confirms profile summary and offers primary buttons.
5) Bot auto-enrolls user into the 7-day challenge (or asks to start now).

2) Home menu (inline buttons)
Persistent keyboard or inline menu message with:
1) Start Workout
2) Daily Challenge
3) Nutrition Tips
4) My Progress
5) My Level
Each button triggers a callback query that routes to the relevant handler. The handler edits the menu message or sends a new structured message to reduce clutter.

3) Daily challenge delivery
1) Scheduler evaluates which users are due at current minute based on preferredWorkoutTime and timezone.
2) For each due user, it checks deliveries for the dateKey to prevent duplicates.
3) It sends:
   1) Day number (1..7)
   2) Workout plan (warm-up, main sets, cooldown, estimated duration)
   3) Nutrition tip (simple, safe, actionable)
   4) Buttons: Start Workout, Mark as Done, Swap/Modify (optional), Remind me later (optional)
4) It records a deliveries entry with messageId.

4) Start workout (button: Start Workout)
1) Bot posts the workout steps in a compact checklist style.
2) Bot provides pacing guidance (rest times, form reminders) and a single “Complete Workout” button.
3) Optionally ask for perceived effort (easy/moderate/hard) after completion to personalize future difficulty.
4) On completion:
   1) Insert workouts log entry (immutable)
   2) Update challengestate streak fields
   3) Add XP and check for rank-up
   4) Send a short celebratory message with tone based on consistency

5) Mark as done (button: Mark as Done)
A fast path for users who did the workout but don’t want the full walkthrough.
1) Create workout completion log with minimal fields.
2) Apply streak and XP updates.
3) Respond with a concise congratulatory message.

6) Nutrition tips (button: Nutrition Tips)
1) Provide a single tip per day, aligned to user goal.
2) Keep tips non-medical and practical.
3) Offer: “Give me another tip” with rate limits, and “Save this tip” (optional, stored in user profile).

7) My progress (button: My Progress)
Show structured snapshot:
1) Current streak and longest streak
2) Workouts completed this week
3) Challenge day status (completed/pending)
4) XP and rank progress
5) Last workout date
Offer buttons: View weekly report (if available), Start workout.

8) My level (button: My Level)
1) Show rank name and what it means.
2) Show XP and next threshold.
3) Show recent achievements (streak milestones, weekly completion badges).

9) Weekly AI summary report
1) Once per week at a configured time (user-local), scheduler creates a sendweekly job.
2) Build a weekly summary input from workouts logs:
   1) total workouts
   2) streak metrics
   3) missed days count
   4) any user feedback on intensity
3) Generate report narrative using AI under strict structure:
   1) Highlights
   2) Consistency score
   3) What to improve next week
   4) Motivation line in FitBuddy X voice
4) Send report and record deliveries.

10) Tone adaptation rules
1) If streak increased or user completed 2+ workouts in last 3 days: upbeat, playful challenge.
2) If no workouts in 3+ days: supportive re-entry, offer a shorter “comeback workout”.
3) If user repeatedly misses preferred time: suggest adjusting preferred time.

11) Failure and fallback behaviors
1) If AI generation fails: use prewritten templates by goal and level.
2) If MongoDB temporarily unavailable: bot replies with a short “try again” message and does not lose user data (ensure writes are retried by request-level logic).
3) If Telegram send fails: mark delivery failed and retry via jobs with backoff and max attempts.
planSections.techStack:
Core runtime:
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot framework.
3) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN read from environment variables.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB for all durable state (users, workouts, challenge state, deliveries, jobs, AI artifacts).
2) In-memory maps allowed only for ephemeral caches (for example, per-process rate limiting counters), not for core state.

AI integration:
1) An external AI text generation API can be used to produce structured plans and weekly reports.
2) The AI layer must be optional behind a feature flag; templates should cover the full functionality if AI is unavailable.
3) Store minimal AI artifacts for debugging while avoiding sensitive data.

Storage:
1) If later adding progress images, meal photos, or PDFs, store in object storage or simple filesystem storage, with MongoDB storing metadata and references.

Deployment and operations:
1) Run as a Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Configure environment variables for token, MongoDB URI, feature flags, and schedule parameters.
3) Use webhook mode when a public HTTPS endpoint is available; otherwise long polling.

Observability:
1) Structured logging (request id, telegramUserId, handler name, latency).
2) Basic metrics counters (messages processed, sends failed, job retries) logged or stored in MongoDB for simple dashboards.

Security:
1) Keep secrets only in environment variables.
2) Validate and sanitize all user inputs.
3) Implement per-user rate limits for AI-heavy endpoints and “another tip” loops.
planSections.nonFunctional:
Reliability and idempotency:
1) Daily/weekly messages must never double-send; deliveries keys enforce this.
2) Scheduler jobs must be restart-safe; jobs collection holds pending work and retry state.
3) All callback actions should be idempotent where possible (for example, completing the same workout twice should be detected by dateKey and challengeDay).

Performance:
1) Keep messages concise to reduce Telegram API overhead.
2) Use indexed queries in MongoDB for telegramUserId, dateKey, deliveries key, jobs runAt.
3) Batch scheduler lookups: query jobs due in the next minute rather than scanning all users.

Privacy:
1) Store only what is needed: basic profile fields and workout logs.
2) Provide a /deletemydata command that purges user data.
3) Avoid storing raw chat history; store only structured summaries when needed.

Safety and compliance posture:
1) Clear disclaimers: general fitness guidance, not medical advice.
2) Conservative defaults: beginner-friendly routines unless user chooses higher intensity.
3) If user indicates injury or pain, recommend stopping and seeking professional advice; provide low-impact alternatives.

Maintainability:
1) Version plan templates (planVersion) to allow updating routines without breaking existing users.
2) Keep domain logic testable (streak calculation, XP thresholds, scheduling key generation).
3) Use feature flags for AI generation, reminders, and extended onboarding.

User experience quality:
1) Structured responses with consistent sections: Today’s workout, Nutrition tip, Quick win, Buttons.
2) Use humor lightly and avoid shaming language.
3) Offer “Remind me later” and “Adjust my time” to reduce churn.

Abuse prevention:
1) Rate limit repeated button taps.
2) Detect spammy usage patterns and degrade gracefully (short replies, block AI calls).
3) Handle group chats by defaulting to private chat onboarding (ask user to DM the bot).

DOCS.md.content:
FitBuddy X – Smart Fitness Coach

What it does

FitBuddy X is an interactive Telegram fitness assistant. It onboards you, runs a 7-day challenge, tracks workouts and streaks, calculates your level, and sends a weekly AI summary report.

Public commands

1) /start
Starts the bot. If you have not completed onboarding, it will ask a few questions (name, age, goal, workout level, preferred workout time). If onboarding is complete, it shows the home menu.

2) /help
Shows how to use the bot and what each menu button does.

3) /reset
Resets your FitBuddy X data (profile, progress, challenge state, workout logs). The bot will ask for confirmation.

Home menu buttons

1) 💪 Start Workout
Gives you a quick workout (even if you are not in the challenge). You can mark it completed and optionally add a note.

2) 🔥 Daily Challenge
Starts or continues a 7-day challenge. If active, it shows today’s plan and provides buttons to complete, swap, skip, or end.

3) 🥗 Nutrition Tips
Shows a simple nutrition tip tailored to your goal and level.

4) 📊 My Progress
Shows total workouts, current streak, best streak, challenge status, and this week’s count. Includes a button to generate the weekly summary on demand.

5) 🏆 My Level
Shows your current level and how many workouts remain until the next level.

Weekly scheduling

The bot runs an in-process scheduler loop. Every cycle it checks which users are due for a weekly AI summary report and sends it at most once per week per user. The bot tracks lastWeeklyReportAt in the progress collection.

Environment variables

Required

1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
Telegram bot token.

2) MONGODB_URI
MongoDB connection string used to store user profiles, progress, challenge state, and workout logs.

3) COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT
CookMyBots AI Gateway base URL. Example: https://api.cookmybots.com/api/ai

4) COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY
CookMyBots AI Gateway key.

Optional

1) TZ
Default timezone used when a user has not configured a timezone. Default is UTC.

2) PORT
Reserved for hosting environments. Default is 3000. The bot does not require HTTP.

3) BASE_URL
Optional display/logging value.

How to test quickly

1) /start
Complete onboarding.

2) Tap 🔥 Daily Challenge
Start the challenge, then tap ✅ Mark Completed.

3) Tap 📊 My Progress
Verify totals and streak.

4) Tap 📩 Get Weekly Summary Now
Verify the AI report is generated.

README.md.content:
FitBuddy X – Smart Fitness Coach

This is a Telegram bot (grammY) that onboards users, runs a 7-day fitness challenge, tracks workouts and streaks, awards levels, and sends weekly AI summary reports.

Setup

1) Install
npm install

2) Configure env
Copy .env.sample to .env and fill in the values.

3) Run locally
npm run dev

4) Run production
npm start

Notes

1) The bot uses long polling by default.
2) MongoDB is required for persistence (profiles, progress, challenge state, workout logs, weekly reports).
3) AI calls go through the CookMyBots AI Gateway (COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT + COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY).

Commands

1) /start
2) /help
3) /reset

Troubleshooting

1) If you see TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is required, set it in your environment.
2) If MongoDB is not configured, the bot will warn and fall back to limited in-memory behavior.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: FitBuddy X – Smart Fitness Coach (Telegram AI Agent)

Summary:
FitBuddy X is a production-ready Telegram bot that behaves like an energetic personal trainer: it onboards users, delivers a 7-day challenge with daily workouts and nutrition tips, tracks workouts and streaks, levels users up, and produces a weekly summary report with motivational analysis.

The bot runs as a single Node.js (ESM) service using grammY. MongoDB is used for persistence (profiles, challenge state, workouts, streaks, level progress, and scheduled report bookkeeping). The design emphasizes reliable state handling, idempotent daily/weekly deliveries, clear user interaction via inline buttons, and safe AI usage with consistent structured outputs and guardrails.

Product goals:
FitBuddy X should help users build consistency through short daily actions, visible progress, and supportive accountability. The product goal is not to be a full exercise library, but to make it easy to start, complete, and track workouts in a lightweight Telegram experience.

Primary outcomes:
1) Increase adherence: daily challenge prompts, streak tracking, and simple completion flows.
2) Increase motivation: leveling system, humor, and adaptive tone that responds to consistency.
3) Increase clarity: structured workout steps, time estimates, rest guidance, and simple nutrition tips.

User promises:
1) A fast onboarding that leads to a personalized plan immediately.
2) One tap to start a workout, one tap to complete it.
3) Progress always available and easy to understand (streak, totals, level, badges).
4) Weekly report that feels personal and constructive, not generic.

Scope boundaries (for production readiness):
1) Provide safe, general fitness guidance with disclaimers; avoid medical advice.
2) Use bodyweight or minimal-equipment routines by default; optionally ask equipment later as an enhancement.
3) Do not require external integrations for wearables or calendars.

High-level architecture:
Runtime and modules:
1) Single Node.js (ESM) process using grammY.
2) Update handling via webhook or long polling depending on deployment; both supported by the same service.
3) MongoDB for durable state; minimal in-memory caching for hot reads only.

Bot interaction layers:
1) Router layer: grammY middleware routes updates into command handlers, callback query handlers (inline buttons), and conversational onboarding.
2) Conversation/state layer: use a conversation approach for onboarding and multi-step flows, backed by MongoDB so it survives restarts.
3) Domain services layer: focused services for user profiles, challenge plan generation, workout logging, streak computation, leveling, and report generation.
4) AI layer (optional but implied by “AI agent”): a constrained generation service that produces workout text, nutrition tips, and weekly report narrative in a strict structured format. The AI layer should be wrapped with:
   1) deterministic prompts that enforce concise sections
   2) validation of outputs (length limits, required fields)
   3) fallbacks to template-based content if generation fails

Scheduling within a single process:

2) Current streak and longest streak
3) XP progress to next rank

   3) Warrior: threshold 2

Key flows:
1) Start and onboarding (/start)
1) User taps /start.
2) Bot checks onboardingStatus.
3) If not completed, begins a guided multi-step conversation:
   1) Ask name
   2) Ask age (validate numeric range; if under a minimum age, restrict intensity and add extra safety messaging)
   3) Ask goal (inline buttons)
   4) Ask workout level (inline buttons)
   5) Ask preferred workout time (ask for HH:mm and clarify it is local time)
   6) Ask timezone offset (simple selection: “I’m in UTC+X”; keep minimal to reduce friction)
4) Bot confirms profile summary and offers primary buttons.
5) Bot auto-enrolls user into the 7-day challenge (or asks to start now).

2) Home menu (inline buttons)
Persistent keyboard or inline menu message with:
1) Start Workout
2) Daily Challenge
3) Nutrition Tips
4) My Progress
5) My Level
Each button triggers a callback query that routes to the relevant handler. The handler edits the menu message or sends a new structured message to reduce clutter.

3) Daily challenge delivery
1) Scheduler evaluates which users are due at current minute based on preferredWorkoutTime and timezone.
2) For each due user, it checks deliveries for the dateKey to prevent duplicates.
3) It sends:
   1) Day number (1..7)
   2) Workout plan (warm-up, main sets, cooldown, estimated duration)
   3) Nutrition tip (simple, safe, actionable)
   4) Buttons: Start Workout, Mark as Done, Swap/Modify (optional), Remind me later (optional)
4) It records a deliveries entry with messageId.

4) Start workout (button: Start Workout)
1) Bot posts the workout steps in a compact checklist style.
2) Bot provides pacing guidance (rest times, form reminders) and a single “Complete Workout” button.
3) Optionally ask for perceived effort (easy/moderate/hard) after completion to personalize future difficulty.
4) On completion:
   1) Insert workouts log entry (immutable)
   2) Update challengestate streak fields
   3) Add XP and check for rank-up
   4) Send a short celebratory message with tone based on consistency

5) Mark as done (button: Mark as Done)
A fast path for users who did the workout but don’t want the full walkthrough.
1) Create workout completion log with minimal fields.
2) Apply streak and XP updates.
3) Respond with a concise congratulatory message.

6) Nutrition tips (button: Nutrition Tips)
1) Provide a single tip per day, aligned to user goal.
2) Keep tips non-medical and practical.
3) Offer: “Give me another tip” with rate limits, and “Save this tip” (optional, stored in user profile).

7) My progress (button: My Progress)
Show structured snapshot:
1) Current streak and longest streak
2) Workouts completed this week
3) Challenge day status (completed/pending)
4) XP and rank progress
5) Last workout date
Offer buttons: View weekly report (if available), Start workout.

8) My level (button: My Level)
1) Show rank name and what it means.
2) Show XP and next threshold.
3) Show recent achievements (streak milestones, weekly completion badges).

9) Weekly AI summary report
1) Once per week at a configured time (user-local), scheduler creates a sendweekly job.
2) Build a weekly summary input from workouts logs:
   1) total workouts
   2) streak metrics
   3) missed days count
   4) any user feedback on intensity
3) Generate report narrative using AI under strict structure:
   1) Highlights
   2) Consistency score
   3) What to improve next week
   4) Motivation line in FitBuddy X voice
4) Send report and record deliveries.

10) Tone adaptation rules
1) If streak increased or user completed 2+ workouts in last 3 days: upbeat, playful challenge.
2) If no workouts in 3+ days: supportive re-entry, offer a shorter “comeback workout”.
3) If user repeatedly misses preferred time: suggest adjusting preferred time.

11) Failure and fallback behaviors
1) If AI generation fails: use prewritten templates by goal and level.
2) If MongoDB temporarily unavailable: bot replies with a short “try again” message and does not lose user data (ensure writes are retried by request-level logic).
3) If Telegram send fails: mark delivery failed and retry via jobs with backoff and max attempts.

Tech stack:
Core runtime:
1) Node.js (ESM) single service/process.
2) grammY for Telegram bot framework.
3) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN read from environment variables.

Persistence:
1) MongoDB for all durable state (users, workouts, challenge state, deliveries, jobs, AI artifacts).
2) In-memory maps allowed only for ephemeral caches (for example, per-process rate limiting counters), not for core state.

AI integration:
1) An external AI text generation API can be used to produce structured plans and weekly reports.
2) The AI layer must be optional behind a feature flag; templates should cover the full functionality if AI is unavailable.
3) Store minimal AI artifacts for debugging while avoiding sensitive data.

Storage:
1) If later adding progress images, meal photos, or PDFs, store in object storage or simple filesystem storage, with MongoDB storing metadata and references.

Deployment and operations:
1) Run as a Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Configure environment variables for token, MongoDB URI, feature flags, and schedule parameters.
3) Use webhook mode when a public HTTPS endpoint is available; otherwise long polling.

Observability:
1) Structured logging (request id, telegramUserId, handler name, latency).
2) Basic metrics counters (messages processed, sends failed, job retries) logged or stored in MongoDB for simple dashboards.

Security:
1) Keep secrets only in environment variables.
2) Validate and sanitize all user inputs.
3) Implement per-user rate limits for AI-heavy endpoints and “another tip” loops.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and idempotency:
1) Daily/weekly messages must never double-send; deliveries keys enforce this.
2) Scheduler jobs must be restart-safe; jobs collection holds pending work and retry state.
3) All callback actions should be idempotent where possible (for example, completing the same workout twice should be detected by dateKey and challengeDay).

Performance:
1) Keep messages concise to reduce Telegram API overhead.
2) Use indexed queries in MongoDB for telegramUserId, dateKey, deliveries key, jobs runAt.
3) Batch scheduler lookups: query jobs due in the next minute rather than scanning all users.

Privacy:
1) Store only what is needed: basic profile fields and workout logs.
2) Provide a /deletemydata command that purges user data.
3) Avoid storing raw chat history; store only structured summaries when needed.

Safety and compliance posture:
1) Clear disclaimers: general fitness guidance, not medical advice.
2) Conservative defaults: beginner-friendly routines unless user chooses higher intensity.
3) If user indicates injury or pain, recommend stopping and seeking professional advice; provide low-impact alternatives.

Maintainability:
1) Version plan templates (planVersion) to allow updating routines without breaking existing users.
2) Keep domain logic testable (streak calculation, XP thresholds, scheduling key generation).
3) Use feature flags for AI generation, reminders, and extended onboarding.

User experience quality:
1) Structured responses with consistent sections: Today’s workout, Nutrition tip, Quick win, Buttons.
2) Use humor lightly and avoid shaming language.
3) Offer “Remind me later” and “Adjust my time” to reduce churn.

Abuse prevention:
1) Rate limit repeated button taps.
2) Detect spammy usage patterns and degrade gracefully (short replies, block AI calls).
3) Handle group chats by defaulting to private chat onboarding (ask user to DM the bot).

Bot commands:
• /start — Start FitBuddy X, run onboarding, and show the main menu. (usage: /start) [Getting started]
• /menu — Show the main action buttons (workout, challenge, nutrition, progress, level). (usage: /menu) [Navigation]
• /challenge — View or start the 7-day fitness challenge and see today’s plan. (usage: /challenge) [Challenge]
• /workout — Start today’s workout (or a quick fallback workout if none scheduled). (usage: /workout) [Workouts]
• /done — Mark today’s workout as completed (fast path). (usage: /done) [Workouts]
• /nutrition — Get today’s nutrition tip tailored to your goal. (usage: /nutrition) [Nutrition]
• /progress — Show streak, totals, and recent activity. (usage: /progress) [Progress]
• /level — Show your rank, XP, and what’s needed to level up. (usage: /level) [Progress]
• /settime — Update your preferred workout time and timezone offset for reminders. (usage: /settime) [Settings]
• /profile — View and edit your profile (goal, level, age). (usage: /profile) [Settings]
• /weekly — View your latest weekly summary report (if available). (usage: /weekly) [Reports]
• /help — Explain how to use FitBuddy X and what commands/buttons do. (usage: /help) [Support]
• /deletemydata — Permanently delete your FitBuddy X data (profile and logs). (usage: /deletemydata) [Privacy]

planSections.productGoals:
FitBuddy X should help users build consistency through short daily actions, visible progress, and supportive accountability. The product goal is not to be a full exercise library, but to make it easy to start, complete, and track workouts in a lightweight Telegram experience.

Primary outcomes:
1) Increase adherence: daily challenge prompts, streak tracking, and simple completion flows.
2) Increase motivation: leveling system, humor, and adaptive tone that responds to consistency.
3) Increase clarity: structured workout steps, time estimates, rest guidance, and simple nutrition tips.

User promises:
1) A fast onboarding that leads to a personalized plan immediately.
2) One tap to start a workout, one tap to complete it.
3) Progress always available and easy to understand (streak, totals, level, badges).
4) Weekly report that feels personal and constructive, not generic.

Scope boundaries (for production readiness):
1) Provide safe, general fitness guidance with disclaimers; avoid medical advice.
2) Use bodyweight or minimal-equipment routines by default; optionally ask equipment later as an enhancement.
3) Do not require external integrations for wearables or calendars.

2) Update handling via webhook or long polling depending on deployment; both supported by the same service.
3) MongoDB for durable state; minimal in-memory caching for hot reads only.

Bot interaction layers:
1) Router layer: grammY middleware routes updates into command handlers, callback query handlers (inline buttons), and conversational onboarding.
2) Conversation/state layer: use a conversation approach for onboarding and multi-step flows, backed by MongoDB so it survives restarts.
3) Domain services layer: focused services for user profiles, challenge plan generation, workout logging, streak computation, leveling, and report generation.
4) AI layer (optional but implied by “AI agent”): a constrained generation service that produces workout text, nutrition tips, and weekly report narrative in a strict structured format. The AI layer should be wrapped with:
   1) deterministic prompts that enforce concise sections
   2) validation of outputs (length limits, required fields)
   3) fallbacks to template-based content if generation fails

envVarDocs.title: Telegram Bot Token
envVarDocs.description: Token used to connect to the Telegram Bot API.
envVarDocs.usage: Required at startup; the bot exits with guidance if missing.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Create a bot with @BotFather on Telegram and copy the token.

envVarDocs.title: MongoDB Connection String
envVarDocs.description: MongoDB connection string used for persistent user profiles, progress, challenges, and workout logs.
envVarDocs.usage: Used on boot to connect and create indexes; if missing, the bot runs but cannot persist state.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Use MongoDB Atlas or your hosted MongoDB provider and copy the connection string.

envVarDocs.title: CookMyBots AI Endpoint
envVarDocs.description: Base URL for CookMyBots AI Gateway. Do not include /chat or other paths.
envVarDocs.usage: Used for weekly summaries and structured fitness content generation.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Provided by CookMyBots.

envVarDocs.title: CookMyBots AI Key
envVarDocs.description: API key for CookMyBots AI Gateway.
envVarDocs.usage: Sent as Authorization: Bearer header on every AI request.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Provided by CookMyBots.

envVarDocs.title: Default Timezone
envVarDocs.description: Fallback timezone used when a user does not have a timezone set.
envVarDocs.usage: Used to compute the date key for streaks and logging.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Pick an IANA timezone like UTC, America/New_York, Europe/London.

envVarDocs.title: Port
envVarDocs.description: Port for hosting environments. This bot does not require HTTP but accepts this env var safely.
envVarDocs.usage: Read at boot for compatibility with hosting platforms.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Your platform sets it automatically (or set 3000 locally).

envVarDocs.title: Base URL (Optional)
envVarDocs.description: Optional base URL for display/logging. Not required for the bot to run.
envVarDocs.usage: Currently only logged as presence boolean.
envVarDocs.howToGet: If deployed, use your public service URL.
